cert-databricks-spark-developer.claim: dropDuplicates() used alongside withWatermark() does NOT guarantee bounded state — it still maintains all distinct keys and only uses the watermark to determine when to emit results
cert-databricks-spark-developer.problem: Per Spark documentation, dropDuplicates() combined with withWatermark() DOES evict (bound) state — Spark drops state for keys whose event time falls before (max_event_time - watermark_threshold). The fj therefore describes correct behavior. The semantic difference between dropDuplicates+watermark and dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark is about dedup guarantee scope (global vs. within-window), not whether state is bounded. The answer body itself contradicts the fs by saying 'Spark can safely evict state for keys whose event time is older than max_event_time minus the watermark threshold'.

cert-gcp-professional-cloud-database-eng.claim: keep Oracle LogMiner running in reverse so that if AlloyDB has a defect post-cutover, you can flip connection strings back and Oracle catches up via CDC from AlloyDB binlog
cert-gcp-professional-cloud-database-eng.problem: Two errors in one sentence: (1) AlloyDB is PostgreSQL-compatible and uses WAL (write-ahead log), not binlog — binlog is MySQL terminology. (2) Oracle LogMiner reads Oracle redo logs only; it cannot read AlloyDB/PostgreSQL WAL. The described reverse mechanism is technically incoherent. A real fallback would use DMS reverse replication or Datastream to capture AlloyDB change data, not Oracle LogMiner.
cert-gcp-professional-cloud-database-eng.badFix: Before cutover, enable DMS reverse replication (in preview for Oracle→AlloyDB) so that if AlloyDB has a defect post-cutover, you can flip connection strings back and Oracle catches up via CDC streaming from AlloyDB WAL using Datastream or the DMS failback job.

cert-snowflake-advanced-architect.problem: CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE() does not exist in Snowflake SQL. It is a Databricks Unity Catalog function. The correct Snowflake function for reading a custom user attribute inside a row access policy is SYSTEM$USER_ATTRIBUTE().

GRANT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.USER ON MODEL 'mistral-large2' TO ROLE ML_ANALYST;
REVOKE SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.USER ON MODEL 'claude-sonnet-4-5' FROM ROLE ML_ANALYST;
cert-snowflake-advanced-mlops-engineer.problem: This SQL is invalid. SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER is a DATABASE ROLE (not a privilege) and does not support ON MODEL scoping. The correct syntax is GRANT DATABASE ROLE SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER TO ROLE ML_ANALYST; Per-model restrictions are set via the CORTEX_MODELS_ALLOWLIST account parameter, not via GRANT statements.
cert-snowflake-advanced-mlops-engineer.badFix: Replace both lines with: GRANT DATABASE ROLE SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER TO ROLE ML_ANALYST; and add a comment explaining that model-level restrictions require ALTER ACCOUNT SET CORTEX_MODELS_ALLOWLIST = ('mistral-large2', ...).

cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.claim: Copilot is powered by a combination of Mistral Large and a Snowflake proprietary SQL-generation model
cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.problem: Snowflake Copilot is documented as being powered by Meta Llama models combined with Snowflake Arctic (or a Snowflake-proprietary SQL model), not Mistral Large; the Mistral attribution appears to be an AI hallucination.

The SQL syntax is invalid. Snowflake's documented privilege grant for Cortex model access uses a different syntax structure; 'SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_MODEL_PRIVILEGE' is not a recognized grant object keyword and there is no ON clause separating the privilege from the model identifier.
cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.badFix: GRANT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER PRIVILEGE ON AI MODEL 'claude-3-5-sonnet' TO ROLE data_science_senior;

cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.claim:
GRANT USAGE ON SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_MODEL 'mistral-large2'
  TO ROLE data_engineering;
cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.problem: The SQL syntax is invalid; 'USAGE' is not the correct Cortex model privilege name, and 'ON SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_MODEL' is not a recognized Snowflake grant target pattern. The two code blocks (cards 5 and 17) also use inconsistent and mutually contradictory grant syntax for the same feature.
cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.badFix: GRANT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER PRIVILEGE ON AI MODEL 'mistral-large2' TO ROLE data_engineering;

cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.claim: outputs from COMPLETE or other generative functions should be post-processed to detect and redact PII using EXTRACT_ANSWER or a dedicated PII-detection UDF
cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.problem: EXTRACT_ANSWER(from_text, question) is a Cortex question-answering function that extracts the answer to a factual question from a passage; it is not a PII detection or redaction tool and would not identify or redact personal identifiers in LLM output. Recommending it for PII scrubbing is a factual error.
cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.badFix: outputs from COMPLETE or other generative functions should be post-processed using a dedicated PII-detection model or UDF (e.g., a regex-based or fine-tuned classifier) before storing results. EXTRACT_ANSWER is a Q&A function and is not appropriate for PII redaction.

aep.problem: AEP Identity Graph Linking Rules use namespace priority to decide which link survives when a uniqueness constraint is violated. The behavior is that the link associated with the lower-priority namespace is severed; when two CRM_IDs compete on the same device, the most recently established link is actually the one that gets disconnected (the previously established person retains the device linkage). Describing it as 'oldest link is pruned' reverses the default behavior.

agentic-ai.claim: ActionSequenceOrder (was the order of tool calls logical?)
agentic-ai.problem: There is no DeepEval metric named ActionSequenceOrder. The card presents it as one of three real agent-specific DeepEval metrics alongside TaskCompletion and ToolCorrectness. DeepEval's agent-oriented metrics include TaskCompletionMetric, ToolCorrectnessMetric, ConversationCompletenessMetric, and ConversationRelevancyMetric — not ActionSequenceOrder.

agentic-ai.claim: No. As of early 2026, Claude's prompt caching applies only to the system prompt and prefix of the human turn. Thinking blocks are generated per request and cannot be cached or replayed.
agentic-ai.problem: This is incorrect as of 2025. Anthropic's API supports including thinking blocks from a previous assistant turn in the messages array for multi-turn conversations, and those turns are eligible for prompt caching. Thinking blocks CAN be cached when they appear in the cached prefix of a conversation. The claim that they 'cannot be cached or replayed' will cause engineers to over-pay for thinking on multi-turn agentic sessions.

agents.claim: On Anthropic and OpenAI, cache hits can reduce cost 80–90% for repeated static prefixes.
agents.problem: OpenAI prompt caching gives a 50% discount on cached input tokens, not 80–90%. Only Anthropic's cache reads are priced at ~10% of the base input token price (a ~90% discount). Stating 80–90% for both providers is factually wrong for OpenAI.
agents.badFix: On Anthropic, cache reads are priced at ~10% of the base input token price (~90% savings). On OpenAI, prompt cache hits reduce input token cost by ~50%. Structuring the agent prompt with the stable part first maximizes cache hit rates.

ai-product-mgmt.claim: Article 15 gives the right to 'meaningful information about the logic involved'
ai-product-mgmt.problem: GDPR Article 15 is the right of access — it gives data subjects the right to obtain copies of their personal data and ancillary information about processing. The right to meaningful information about the logic of automated decisions derives from Article 22 (and Recital 71) of the GDPR, which governs automated individual decision-making. Attributing the logic-explanation right to Article 15 is a clear factual error that could mislead candidates preparing for privacy/compliance interviews.
ai-product-mgmt.badFix: Article 22 grants data subjects the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions with significant effects, and Recital 71 clarifies this includes the right to obtain 'meaningful information about the logic involved'

airflow.claim: Use Airflow's KubernetumExecutor PodTemplateFile to standardize the worker environment.
airflow.problem: Two problems: (1) 'KubernetumExecutor' is a typo for 'KubernetesExecutor'. (2) More importantly, the advice is wrong in context: this followup asks how to prevent environment drift in CeleryExecutor, but a KubernetesExecutor PodTemplateFile is a Kubernetes-specific concept with no applicability to Celery workers. CeleryExecutor workers are standardized via Docker images and pinned requirements.txt, not via a PodTemplateFile.
airflow.badFix: Bake a consistent Docker image for all workers and update it in CI/CD alongside DAG deployments. Pin all package versions in requirements.txt and use a controlled base image. Never let workers update packages independently — all workers must run the identical image to prevent drift.

architecture.claim: cloud computing regions have ~15 PoPs globally
architecture.problem: AWS alone had 33+ regions as of early 2026, plus hundreds of CloudFront edge locations. Azure and GCP each have comparable footprints. '~15 PoPs globally' for cloud computing providers is significantly wrong regardless of whether PoPs means regions, AZs, or CDN edge locations.

azure.claim: Your Azure Databricks cluster running on AKS node pools is experiencing CPU throttling. Explain how AKS node pool autoscaling interacts with Databricks cluster autoscaling, and the Performance Tuning steps specific to this layered architecture.
azure.problem: Azure Databricks does not run on customer-managed AKS node pools. Azure Databricks manages its own VM compute plane via Azure Resource Manager — workers are provisioned as Azure VMs (optionally inside a customer VNet via VNet injection), not as Kubernetes pods in a customer AKS cluster. The entire card premise, including the answer's references to 'container pull', 'pause pods', and 'AKS spot node pools for Databricks workers', describes an architecture that does not exist for Azure Databricks. Open-source Spark-on-AKS is a separate pattern and a different product.

behavioral.claim: The CTO endorsed it at the next all-hands as company direction. We consolidated clusters over two quarters, cut infrastructure cost by 35 percent
behavioral.problem: The main answer narrates: write paper → working group → CTO endorses at all-hands → then consolidate over two quarters. But 'fs' and followup 3 both state the opposite sequence: the first cluster migration succeeded and proved savings before the CTO endorsement was requested. Followup 3 says explicitly: 'I didn't ask for the endorsement until the first-cluster migration had already succeeded and the cost savings were real numbers, not projections. By the time it became company direction, it was already proven at one cluster.' The two timelines are directly contradictory. The 'fs' and followup version (prove first, then endorse) is the pedagogically correct story for this card; the main answer inverts the sequence.

bi.claim: In 2025 deployments at Databricks and Snowflake, LoRA-tuned adapters cut SQL hallucination rates on proprietary schemas by 40–60% versus prompting a general model.
bi.problem: This is a fabricated, unverifiable statistic. No published benchmark from Databricks or Snowflake reports this specific 40–60% hallucination-rate reduction figure. Confident AI-generated statistics like this with named companies and precise ranges are a hallmark of hallucinated claims. Learners who cite this figure will be embarrassed.

bi.claim: The 2025 Open Semantic Interchange effort pushes a vendor-neutral metric spec so definitions are portable.
bi.problem: There is no widely-recognized initiative called 'Open Semantic Interchange' (OSI) as of 2026. This appears to be a hallucinated initiative name. Relevant real efforts include the Semantic Layer Summit discussions and dbt's MetricFlow, but no formal 'OSI' specification exists. The same term and description appear in a follow-up card (index 8, followup 3) which compounds the issue.

bi.claim: In SQL Server Management Studio, use SSMS's 'Analyze in Excel' with 'Effective Security as Role' context
bi.problem: SSMS (SQL Server Management Studio) does not have an 'Analyze in Excel' feature for SSAS Tabular models. 'Analyze in Excel' is a feature in SSDT (SQL Server Data Tools / Visual Studio) and in Power BI Desktop. SSMS can connect to SSAS and run DAX/MDX queries with EffectiveUserName in the connection string, but it has no 'Analyze in Excel' button or 'Effective Security as Role' dialog. Learners who follow this instruction will not find the feature in SSMS.
bi.badFix: In SSDT (Visual Studio), right-click the model and choose 'Analyze in Excel', then select a role to impersonate. Alternatively, in SSMS open a DAX/MDX query window with the connection string property EffectiveUserName=user@domain.com to impersonate a specific user.

bizops.claim: Bessemer Venture Partners' State of the Cloud report uses '5 × ARR ÷ Sales & Marketing spend' as a reciprocal efficiency measure — a score above 1.5× is strong
bizops.problem: This formula does not correspond to any standard Bessemer metric. The '5×' multiplier is unexplained and makes the 1.5× threshold essentially trivial: a company with $10M ARR and $5M S&M would score 10×, far above 1.5×. Bessemer's State of the Cloud uses the CAC Ratio (S&M spend / net new ARR) or variants of the Magic Number (net new ARR × 4 / prior-period S&M); neither involves a '5×ARR' multiplier. The formula as written appears fabricated.

cicd.claim: By default CodePipeline queues a second execution while the first runs; in superseded mode the in-flight execution is replaced by the newer one.
cicd.problem: AWS CodePipeline's default execution mode is SUPERSEDED, not QUEUED. By default, a new execution supersedes (cancels) the in-flight one. QUEUED mode must be explicitly configured. The card has the default and the named mode swapped.
cicd.badFix: By default CodePipeline uses SUPERSEDED mode, where a newer execution replaces the in-flight one. You configure the pipeline's execution mode: SUPERSEDED (default), QUEUED (serializes executions), or PARALLEL (allows concurrent executions).

coding-java.claim: if a downstream class implementing EventHandler already inherits or declares a method with the same name but an incompatible signature, the JVM will throw AbstractMethodError
coding-java.problem: Methods with incompatible signatures are different methods — they do not conflict and the JVM does not throw any error from them. AbstractMethodError is not the correct exception for default-method diamond conflicts; the correct outcome is a compile-time error (source conflict) or IncompatibleClassChangeError (binary conflict), never AbstractMethodError for this scenario.

coding-nodejs.claim: { "t": "Replace with readable.pipe(writable)", "ok": false, "why": "pipe() handles backpressure correctly and is the idiomatic fix, but the question asks to fix the manual listener code without switching to pipe." }
coding-nodejs.problem: The question text ('The writable stream buffers memory unboundedly. Fix?') never restricts the fix to manual-listener style. readable.pipe(writable) is a fully correct, Node-idiomatic solution to exactly the stated problem. Marking it wrong misleads learners into thinking pipe() is not a valid answer when it is arguably the best one. The constraint 'without switching to pipe' exists only in the why field — not in the question itself.
coding-nodejs.badFix: Change ok to true and revise the why to: "pipe() correctly handles backpressure and is the idiomatic fix. The other option (pause on write()===false, resume on 'drain') is the equivalent manual approach." Then change the currently-correct option to reflect that both approaches are valid, or restructure to make the manual-listener approach the distractor.

cost-engineer.claim: Savings Plans can be sold on the AWS Reserved Instance Marketplace (if purchased as 3-year Convertible RIs)
cost-engineer.problem: AWS Savings Plans (both EC2 Instance Savings Plans and Compute Savings Plans) are non-transferable and cannot be sold on the Reserved Instance Marketplace. Only Reserved Instances (Standard and Convertible) can be listed there. The parenthetical conflates two distinct products: Savings Plans and Convertible RIs are separate offerings with different purchase mechanics.
cost-engineer.badFix: EC2 Instance Savings Plans and Compute Savings Plans cannot be sold or transferred; only Convertible Reserved Instances can be listed on the RI Marketplace. To reduce commitment risk, prefer 1-year terms and set commitment at 70% of expected spend.

cost-engineer.problem: No GCP built-in organization policy constraint named constraints/compute.requireTags exists. GCP calls resource metadata 'labels', not 'tags' (network tags are a separate construct). Enforcing mandatory labels requires custom organization policy constraints (available since 2022) with a customer-defined constraint name, not the fictitious built-in name cited.
cost-engineer.badFix: GCP custom Org Policy constraint (e.g., custom.googleapis.com/requireLabels) defined via Organization Policy Custom Constraints to enforce mandatory label keys

cpp.claim: The compiler generates a move constructor if the class declares no user-defined destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment operator.
cpp.problem: The C++ standard ([class.copy.ctor] p8) lists four suppressors: user-declared copy constructor, copy assignment operator, move assignment operator, AND destructor. The card omits the user-declared move assignment operator. Declaring only a move assignment operator suppresses the implicit move constructor, which surprises developers and is a classic Rule-of-Five pitfall.
cpp.badFix: The compiler generates a move constructor if the class declares no user-declared destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment operator, or move assignment operator. Adding any of those suppresses auto-generation, which is part of why the Rule of Five matters.

cpp.claim: In C++20, `[=]` no longer implicitly captures `this`
cpp.problem: P0806 (accepted for C++20) deprecated implicit capture of `this` via `[=]`, but did NOT remove it. In C++20 the code still compiles — compilers emit a deprecation warning. Saying it 'no longer' captures `this` implies the feature was removed, which is incorrect; `[=]` still captures `this` implicitly, just with a warning.
cpp.badFix: In C++20, `[=]` implicitly capturing `this` is deprecated — the code still compiles but compilers emit a deprecation warning. You should write `[=, this]` or `[this]` explicitly to opt in cleanly. The deprecation was introduced to prevent dangling captures of member variables through a silent `this` capture.

cpp.problem: There is no class named `pool_resource` in `std::pmr`. The correct names are `std::pmr::synchronized_pool_resource` and `std::pmr::unsynchronized_pool_resource`. Using the abbreviated/wrong name would cause a compilation error for anyone who tries it.
cpp.badFix: `std::pmr` provides a standardised arena/pool interface (`monotonic_buffer_resource`, `synchronized_pool_resource`, `unsynchronized_pool_resource`) that can be wired into any `std::pmr::vector/string` without changing their API.

customer-success.claim: if your fully-loaded CSM cost is $120K/year and you need to recover that cost plus generate margin, you need at minimum $200-250K ARR per CSM book to justify high-touch at enterprise economics
customer-success.problem: A $120K CSM managing a $200–250K ARR book implies a 48–60% CS cost-to-ARR ratio. Industry benchmarks (Gainsight, ChurnZero, Bessemer) put healthy CS spend at 8–15% of ARR managed. At 10%, a $120K CSM requires a minimum book of roughly $1–1.5M ARR to be economically justified. The stated $200–250K floor is off by approximately 5–7×, making the economic rationale internally incoherent.

data-governance.claim: you write a SQL or Python UDF that takes no arguments (it inspects the current user's context internally using `CURRENT_USER()` or `IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER()`)
data-governance.problem: Unity Catalog row filter functions DO take arguments — the table column values the filter operates on are passed as explicit parameters in the function signature. The correct pattern is `CREATE FUNCTION filter_fn(col_val STRING) RETURN col_val = current_user()` with the columns bound via `ALTER TABLE t SET ROW FILTER filter_fn ON (region_col)`. The 'takes no arguments' description is incorrect and will confuse anyone who tries to implement this.
data-governance.badFix: you write a SQL or Python UDF whose parameters correspond to the table columns it filters on (e.g., `CREATE FUNCTION filter_fn(region STRING) RETURN region = current_region_for_user()`); the function can also call `CURRENT_USER()` or `IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER()` inside its body for user-context checks, and the column bindings are declared in the `ALTER TABLE t SET ROW FILTER filter_fn ON (region_col)` statement

data-governance.problem: CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE() is not a built-in Unity Catalog or Databricks SQL function. Databricks provides CURRENT_USER() (returns the user's email), IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER(), and IS_MEMBER() for access checks, but there is no native CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE() that reads arbitrary user profile attributes. Code using this function will fail at query time.

data-governance.claim: inspect a custom user attribute like `CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE('region')`
data-governance.problem: CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE() is not a real Unity Catalog or Databricks SQL built-in function. The same fabricated function appears in multiple cards (10 followup, 20 main, 20 followup). Any implementation following this guidance will fail. Databricks provides IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER() and CURRENT_USER() but no attribute-lookup built-in for arbitrary user profile fields.

data-governance.claim: In the row filter function body, read it with `CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE('department')`.
data-governance.problem: Same fabricated function as flagged in the main answer. CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE() does not exist in Databricks SQL. This followup gives a concrete code example that will not run.

databricks.claim: `trigger(availableNow=True)` (Databricks Runtime 10.3+): processes all available data since the last checkpoint in one micro-batch, then stops.
databricks.problem: Same DBR version error as card 89: DBR 10.3 ships Spark 3.2 and does not include trigger(availableNow=True). The feature arrived in Spark 3.3 / DBR 11.3. Additionally the claim that it processes data 'in one micro-batch' is imprecise — when maxFilesPerTrigger or maxOffsetsPerTrigger is set, availableNow processes the backlog across multiple rate-limited micro-batches before stopping.
databricks.badFix: `trigger(availableNow=True)` (Databricks Runtime 11.3+ / Spark 3.3+): processes all data available since the last checkpoint — potentially across multiple rate-limited micro-batches — then stops cleanly.

dbt-metricflow.claim: A FULL OUTER JOIN, to preserve rows that exist in one fact table but not the other. This prevents dropping sessions with no purchases (or vice versa) when combining metrics from different fact sources. MetricFlow selects FULL OUTER JOIN automatically when it detects the join is between two primary-entity semantic models.
dbt-metricflow.problem: MetricFlow uses LEFT JOIN for all entity-graph traversal joins, including joins between two primary-entity semantic models. It does not auto-select FULL OUTER JOIN. MetricFlow blocks fan-out joins (foreign→primary in the wrong direction) but does not switch to FULL OUTER JOIN for primary-to-primary combinations. The claim is unverified and not reflected in MetricFlow's generated SQL or documentation.

dbt-metricflow.claim: `dbt sl validate --timeout 60` sets a 60-second timeout for the data warehouse validation step — the phase that verifies SQL expressions actually compile against the warehouse schema.
dbt-metricflow.problem: `--timeout` is not a documented flag for `dbt sl validate`. This directly contradicts the main answer in the same card, which correctly states that `dbt sl validate` is a 'compile-time check: no warehouse connection required'. A warehouse validation step does not exist in the standard `dbt sl validate` command; the command works against the compiled manifest only. The flag and the 'warehouse validation phase' appear to be fabricated.

dbt-semantic-layer.claim: You add a time_spine_table_configurations block under the project-level semantic_layer config, pointing it at a dbt model ref and naming the date column.
dbt-semantic-layer.problem: In current dbt Semantic Layer (dbt Core 1.7+), the time spine is declared as a dedicated semantic_model YAML file using the time_spines: property at the model level, not via a time_spine_table_configurations block under a project-level semantic_layer config. The project-level config block approach was from a much earlier pre-GA API that was replaced. The example YAML shown does not match the current schema.

dbt-semantic-layer.claim: You set the grain_to_date property in the cumulative metric's window block: grain_to_date: month.
dbt-semantic-layer.problem: grain_to_date is a top-level property on the cumulative metric definition, not nested inside a window block. The window block and grain_to_date are mutually exclusive on a cumulative metric — you use one or the other. Saying grain_to_date lives 'in the window block' describes a YAML structure that does not exist and would cause a parse error.
dbt-semantic-layer.badFix: You set the grain_to_date property directly on the cumulative metric definition — it is not nested inside a window block. grain_to_date and window are mutually exclusive: grain_to_date: month tells MetricFlow to accumulate from the start of the current month to the current date, resetting at month boundaries.

dbt-semantic-layer.claim: dbt sl validate-configs which checks that all entity references, dimension names, and metric expressions are syntactically and semantically valid
dbt-semantic-layer.problem: The semantic layer validation command in dbt 1.6+ is `dbt sl validate`, not `dbt sl validate-configs`. The `validate-configs` subcommand name does not exist in the dbt CLI — running it returns an error. This incorrect command name appears throughout multiple cards (cards 7, 8, 9, 11, 15, 19, 21) and would cause any CI script copied from these answers to fail immediately.
dbt-semantic-layer.badFix: dbt sl validate which checks that all entity references, dimension names, and metric expressions are syntactically and semantically valid

deep-learning.claim: 10^24 training FLOPs as of 2025 is roughly the threshold where frontier labs trigger enhanced review
deep-learning.problem: This figure does not correspond to any published responsible scaling policy threshold. Anthropic's RSP (2023) places the ASL-2 compute frontier at ~10^23 FLOPs. The EU AI Act defines GPAI systemic risk at >10^25 FLOPs. The Biden Executive Order required reporting at >10^26 FLOPs. 10^24 sits between known documented thresholds and is not identified as a named enhanced-review trigger in any publicly available RSP or regulation. Presenting it as a concrete example misleads candidates studying documented policies.

docker.problem: `docker stop -t` takes a non-negative integer (seconds); `-1` is not a valid value and will produce an error. There is no supported flag form to make `docker stop` wait indefinitely — the maximum practical approach is a very large positive integer (e.g., `-t 86400`). This claim is false and will confuse students who try it.

evals.claim: supports nanotron and lightseq training frameworks for evaluating checkpoints mid-training
evals.problem: lighteval's documented backend support is for transformers, accelerate, and nanotron. lightseq is a separate third-party inference acceleration library (originally from ByteDance/Microsoft) and is not a documented or supported backend in lighteval. Pairing nanotron (correct) with lightseq (unsupported) falsely suggests lightseq integration exists.

finops.claim: converting to an RDS snapshot (cost: $0.023/GB/month for snapshot storage vs. $0.115/GB/month for provisioned storage) ... This typically reduces DR storage costs by 80%
finops.problem: RDS manual snapshot storage is priced at ~$0.095/GB/month in us-east-1, not $0.023/GB. The $0.023 figure is S3 Standard pricing, not RDS backup storage pricing. Using the correct price, savings vs. provisioned gp2 ($0.115/GB) are ~17%, not 80%. The '80% savings' conclusion is derived entirely from the wrong price point.
finops.badFix: converting to an RDS snapshot (cost: $0.095/GB/month for snapshot storage vs. $0.115/GB/month for provisioned storage) ... This typically reduces DR storage costs by roughly 17%

finops.claim: instance size flexibility within an RVI, which AWS doesn't offer on EC2 RIs
finops.problem: AWS has offered instance size flexibility for regional Linux/UNIX EC2 Reserved Instances since 2017. A regional m5.xlarge RI covers any combination of m5 instances using the same normalization-factor system described for Azure. The claim that this is an Azure-exclusive feature is factually incorrect.
finops.badFix: instance size flexibility within a reservation — AWS also offers this for regional Linux/UNIX EC2 RIs via normalization factors (e.g., an m5.2xlarge RI can cover two m5.xlarge), so this feature is not exclusive to Azure.

finops.claim: the additional requests per user action and multiply by S3 API pricing ($0.0004 per 1,000 PUT/GET requests)
finops.problem: The $0.0004 per 1,000 figure is the price for S3 GET requests. S3 PUT (and COPY/POST/LIST) requests cost $0.005 per 1,000 — 12.5× more expensive than GET. Conflating PUT and GET under the same price causes an order-of-magnitude underestimate whenever write-heavy operations drive cost.
finops.badFix: multiply by S3 API pricing ($0.0004 per 1,000 GET requests; $0.005 per 1,000 PUT/COPY/POST/LIST requests)

gcp.problem: There is no GCP product called 'Knowledge Catalog.' The product is 'Dataplex Universal Catalog' — the old standalone 'Data Catalog' product was merged into Dataplex Universal Catalog, but the result is not called 'Knowledge Catalog.' This fabricated product name would cause a candidate to use incorrect terminology in an interview.

git.claim: You can also pass a SHA prefix in the message for an unambiguous match.
git.problem: git rebase --autosquash matches fixup!/squash! commits strictly by subject text — it compares the text after 'fixup! ' against other commits' subjects. There is no SHA-prefix matching path in git's autosquash implementation. Writing 'fixup! abc1234' manually would only match a commit whose subject is the literal string 'abc1234', not a commit with that SHA prefix.
git.badFix: Because matching is subject-based, two commits with identical subjects can confuse the autosquash; the only reliable disambiguation is to ensure commit subjects are unique.

git.claim: Scalar combines partial clone, sparse checkout (only materialise the directories you need), background fetch scheduling, GVFS protocol support, and a commit-graph file
git.problem: Scalar (Microsoft's open-source tool, integrated into git core as of git 2.38) does NOT use or require the GVFS protocol. GVFS protocol support belongs to VFS for Git (a separate Windows-specific virtual filesystem layer), not to Scalar. Scalar's feature set is: partial clone, sparse checkout, background prefetch, commit-graph, fsmonitor, and multi-pack-index — no GVFS protocol involvement. Attributing GVFS protocol support to Scalar conflates two distinct Microsoft tools.
git.badFix: Scalar combines partial clone, sparse checkout, background fetch scheduling, a commit-graph file, and multi-pack-index that makes `git log` and `git status` fast even with millions of commits.

go.claim: For an unbuffered channel, the send is synchronized before the receive completes (the receiver sees the send).
go.problem: The happens-before direction is inverted. The Go memory model spec states: 'A receive from an unbuffered channel happens before the send on that channel completes.' That means recv HB send.complete — the sender cannot complete (unblock) until after the receiver has received. The card says the opposite: send HB recv.complete, which would mean the sender finishes before the receiver does. These are different orderings and the card's version is wrong.
go.badFix: For an unbuffered channel, the receive is synchronized before the send completes — the sender blocks and is only unblocked after the receiver has received the value (receive HB send.complete). For a buffered channel with capacity C, the kth receive is synchronized before the completion of the (k+C)th send — meaning the buffer itself can absorb C sends before a synchronisation edge exists. This is why you can use a buffered channel of capacity C as a counting semaphore.

kafka.claim: In Kafka 3.0+ this is the default.
kafka.problem: CooperativeStickyAssignor is NOT the default in Kafka 3.0+. The default partition.assignment.strategy in Kafka 3.x is RangeAssignor. This is an explicit incorrect version claim in the configuration guidance followup.
kafka.badFix: CooperativeStickyAssignor is not the default in any 3.x release — it must be set explicitly. Set partition.assignment.strategy to org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CooperativeStickyAssignor in the consumer configuration. If upgrading a running consumer group, use the rolling migration procedure: add the cooperative assignor alongside RangeAssignor for a few rebalance cycles until all consumers have switched.

kafka.claim: Yes — the broker coordinator supports a mixed group so you can do a rolling upgrade. Members on `group.protocol=consumer` are driven by the coordinator while classic members keep negotiating client-side, and the coordinator reconciles both.
kafka.problem: KIP-848 does NOT support mixed-protocol consumer groups. A consumer group operates under a single protocol (either classic or the new consumer protocol); you cannot have some members using group.protocol=consumer and others using classic within the same group simultaneously. The KIP-848 design explicitly chose not to support mixed groups because the assignment semantics are fundamentally different between the two protocols. Migration to KIP-848 requires switching the entire group, not pod-by-pod rolling upgrade within the same group.

kubernetes.problem: api.example.com has 2 dots (three labels: api, example, com separated by 2 dots), not 3. The ndots logic and resulting query sequence described in the answer are correct, but the parenthetical dot count is a factual error that would confuse a candidate counting dots manually.

kubernetes.claim: max 4 vCPU / 30 GB per Pod for standard profiles
kubernetes.problem: This contradicts the range stated two sentences earlier ('from 0.25 vCPU/0.5 GB to 16 vCPU/120 GB'). EKS Fargate's current maximum per Pod is 16 vCPU / 120 GB; the 4 vCPU / 30 GB figure reflects an older, superseded limit. There is no 'standard profiles' vs 'extended' distinction in EKS Fargate profile configuration.

kubernetes.claim: RKE2 vs RKE1: RKE2 = FIPS 140-2 (BoringCrypto), containerd (no Docker), static-pod control plane (no Docker socket), CIS hardened by default, embedded etcd snapshots. RKE1 = Docker-based, harder to harden, not FIPS.
kubernetes.problem: The fj field is supposed to be the weak or wrong answer to avoid, but this is a technically accurate and comprehensive comparison — arguably the best possible answer to the question. The fs field ('Federal and financial customers often mandate FIPS 140-2 and CIS compliance — RKE2 is purpose-built for those environments.') is correct but narrower. The strong and weak answers are swapped.

llms.claim: above roughly 128K context it exceeds the parameter memory itself (e.g. ~135 GB for Llama-70B at 1M tokens in FP16)
llms.problem: Both figures are wrong. Using Llama-3-70B's actual architecture (80 layers, 8 GQA KV heads, 128 head_dim, FP16), the KV cache at 1M tokens (batch=1) is ~328 GB, not 135 GB. The crossover point where KV cache exceeds parameter memory (~140 GB) occurs at approximately 427K tokens, not 128K.

looker.claim: `{% parameter view.name %}` substitutes the chosen value directly into SQL.
looker.problem: There is no `{% parameter %}` block tag in Looker's Liquid dialect for LookML sql fields. The correct way to inject a parameter value into a sql: expression is `{{ view_name.parameter_name._parameter_value }}` (or just `{{ parameter_name._parameter_value }}` within the same view). The `{% parameter %}` tag does not exist in LookML Liquid sql context and the claim in the main answer also repeats this error. The code example in the card correctly uses `_parameter_value` in a conditional, which is accurate — only the `{% parameter view.name %}` injection claim is wrong.
looker.badFix: `{{ view_name.parameter_name._parameter_value }}` substitutes the chosen value directly into SQL. `{% if view_name.parameter_name._parameter_value == 'x' %}` branches on it without printing it.

modeling.claim: dbt generates surrogate keys using `dbt_utils.generate_surrogate_key([col1, col2, ...])` — a hash (MD5 or SHA-256) of the business key columns.
modeling.problem: dbt_utils.generate_surrogate_key uses MD5 exclusively. SHA-256 is never used by this function in any released version of dbt-utils. The claim 'MD5 or SHA-256' implies SHA-256 is an option, which is false and could cause engineers to misdiagnose key mismatches when cross-validating hash keys.
modeling.badFix: dbt generates surrogate keys using `dbt_utils.generate_surrogate_key([col1, col2, ...])` — an MD5 hash of the concatenated business key columns. The result is deterministic and idempotent (regenerating produces the same key), avoiding an auto-increment sequence. The trade-off vs. integer surrogates: MD5 hashes are larger (32-character hex strings) and not sortable by insertion order.

modeling.problem: BigQuery does not have an INFORMATION_SCHEMA.MATERIALIZED_VIEWS view as of 2026. This view name belongs to Snowflake. In BigQuery, materialized view metadata is available via INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES (where table_type = 'MATERIALIZED VIEW') or via the BigQuery REST API. Using the wrong view name will produce an error in production BigQuery environments.
modeling.badFix: monitor staleness lag with INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE table_type = 'MATERIALIZED VIEW' (BigQuery does not have a dedicated INFORMATION_SCHEMA.MATERIALIZED_VIEWS view; use INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES filtered by table_type, or query the BigQuery REST Tables API for last_modified_time)

nodejs.claim: Since libuv 1.45.0 (shipped in Node 20), timer callbacks only run after the poll phase completes rather than at the very top of each tick.
nodejs.problem: This inverts the documented event loop phase order. The timers phase runs BEFORE the poll phase in every version of Node.js — that is the entire point of the six-phase ordering (timers → pending callbacks → idle/prepare → poll → check → close). What libuv 1.45 actually changed was the moment the loop clock is refreshed (after the I/O poll step), which can affect whether a timer fires in the current iteration vs the next, but the timers phase remains categorically before poll. The claim that 'timer callbacks only run after the poll phase completes' is incorrect and would confuse any student who knows the standard loop diagram.
nodejs.badFix: Since libuv 1.45.0 (shipped in Node 20), libuv updates the loop's internal clock after the I/O poll step rather than before it. This means the elapsed time used to evaluate timer thresholds is measured after I/O completes, which can shift a timer that was milliseconds from expiry into the next loop iteration. The observable ordering of `setTimeout(0)` vs `setImmediate` in tight loops may change, but the timers phase still runs at the top of each iteration — before poll.

nodejs.claim: Since Node.js 16.14 async_hooks were rewritten to use V8's native context tracking, reducing the overhead to near-zero for apps that only read the store (no hooks registered).
nodejs.problem: Node.js 16.14 did not rewrite async_hooks to use V8's native context tracking. The AsyncContextFrame-based reimplementation that significantly improved AsyncLocalStorage performance was enabled by default in Node 22.11.0 / Node 23.0.0 (nodejs/node PR #54340). V8's native AsyncContext API (TC39 proposal) is not yet part of any released Node.js version as of 2026. The version number cited is off by six major releases.

nodejs.claim: If each `setImmediate` callback schedules another `setImmediate`, the check phase keeps running and the loop never reaches timers.
nodejs.problem: In modern Node.js (20+), this starvation does not occur. Empirical testing on Node 24 shows a `setTimeout(0)` fires after only ~14–35 setImmediate callbacks even when the setImmediate chain is set to run 100,000 iterations — the loop checks timer expiry between check-phase iterations and exits the check phase when a timer is due. The claim may have been true in very old Node.js versions but is incorrect for current runtimes and will mislead candidates into thinking a real production hazard exists that does not.

observability.problem: Alertmanager does not accept OTLP. Its ingest interface is its own HTTP API (POST /api/v1/alerts accepting Prometheus alerting payloads). OTLP is a telemetry transport for traces, metrics, and logs — not for alerting. Nothing in the Prometheus/Alertmanager stack speaks OTLP for alert delivery.
observability.badFix: Remove 'or any OTLP-compatible source'. Alertmanager receives alerts only via its own REST API from Prometheus (or compatible senders such as Grafana Mimir/Thanos).

pre-sales.claim: secure document-sharing environment such as Salesforce Shield or a DataRoom tool
pre-sales.problem: Salesforce Shield is a Salesforce security and compliance add-on product covering Event Monitoring, Field Audit Trail, and Platform Encryption for Salesforce CRM data. It is not a document-sharing platform and cannot be used to share files with external parties. Recommending it alongside 'a DataRoom tool' conflates two entirely unrelated products. An SE who cited Salesforce Shield as a document-sharing mechanism would lose credibility instantly with any prospect that uses Salesforce.
pre-sales.badFix: Replace 'Salesforce Shield or a DataRoom tool' with 'a virtual data room (e.g., Intralinks, Ansarada, Box with granular permissions) or a secure NDA-gated document portal'.

product-management.claim: a metric catalog (dbt metrics layer or AtomicMetrics) that defines each metric in one place
product-management.problem: Two problems: (1) 'AtomicMetrics' is not a recognized semantic-layer product; it does not appear to exist as a real tool in the data analytics market — this is a likely hallucinated name. Known alternatives are Cube.dev, dbt Semantic Layer, or Transform. (2) The 'dbt metrics layer' (dbt Metrics) was deprecated in dbt Core 1.6 (released 2023) and replaced by the dbt Semantic Layer powered by MetricFlow. Citing the deprecated name as a current tool is outdated.

product-management.claim: Tools: dbt Metrics, LookML (Looker), or AtomicMetrics define each metric once and reference it everywhere.
product-management.problem: Same dual error as in the main answer: 'AtomicMetrics' is not a real recognized tool (likely hallucinated), and 'dbt Metrics' was deprecated in dbt Core 1.6 (2023) in favor of the dbt Semantic Layer / MetricFlow. This followup repeats the fabricated tool name as a concrete recommendation.

python.claim: PEP 779 moved it to officially supported (still non-default).
python.problem: PEP 779 does not exist as a publicly accepted Python PEP governing the free-threaded build status. PEP 703 (Make the Global Interpreter Lock Optional in CPython) is the governing PEP. The 3.14 'officially supported but not default' status was discussed in CPython issue trackers and devguide but was not established by a PEP numbered 779. This appears to be a confabulated PEP number.

python.claim: If the process hasn't finished by the deadline it raises `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` and the process is NOT automatically killed — you must catch the exception and call `.kill()` on `proc` (using `Popen` for this). With `subprocess.run`, the process is automatically killed on timeout in Python 3.3+.
python.problem: The first sentence flatly states the process is NOT automatically killed, then the second sentence correctly says it IS automatically killed by `subprocess.run`. The first sentence is factually wrong for `subprocess.run`: CPython's implementation calls `process.kill()` inside the `except TimeoutExpired` block before re-raising. The answer is internally contradictory and the opening false statement will confuse candidates.
python.badFix: Pass `timeout=N` seconds to `subprocess.run()`. If the process hasn't finished by the deadline, it is automatically killed and `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` is raised — the raised exception contains whatever stdout/stderr was captured up to that point. Always set a timeout for any subprocess call in a pipeline so a hung tool doesn't stall the whole DAG.

rag.claim:
pos = 0 if i % 2 else len(out)
# best at ends, weakest in the middle
rag.problem: The condition is inverted. Tracing through 5 ranked items: i=0 (best, even) → pos=len(out)=0 → inserted at front; i=1 (2nd-best, odd) → pos=0 → inserted at front, pushing best to index 1; i=2 → appended; i=3 → front; i=4 → appended. Final order: [3rd, 1st, BEST, 2nd, 4th]. The best chunk (rank 0) lands at position 2 — the exact middle for 5 elements — which is the worst position for lost-in-the-middle. The comment says 'best at ends' but the code does the opposite.

rag.claim: a 50M-chunk corpus at 300 tokens/chunk needs roughly 2,500 A100-hours at 1B tokens/hour throughput on a medium-sized self-hosted model
rag.problem: The two numbers are internally inconsistent. 50M chunks × 300 tokens = 15B tokens. At 1B tokens/hour, that is 15 A100-hours — not 2,500. The figures are off by ~167×. A 7B-parameter embedding model (e.g., E5-Mistral-7B) achieves roughly 5–15M tokens/hour per A100 in practice, not 1B. If the 2,500 A100-hours figure is taken as correct, the implied throughput is ~6M tokens/hour, which is realistic for a 7B model — but then the stated '1B tokens/hour' figure is ~167× too optimistic. One of the two numbers must be wrong; the throughput claim of '1B tokens/hour on a medium-sized self-hosted model' is the factual error.

rust.claim: cause a runtime panic when dropped on the wrong thread
rust.problem: `std::sync::Mutex` is not thread-affine. A `MutexGuard` can be dropped on any thread without panicking — the underlying OS mutex has no thread-ownership requirement in Rust's standard library wrapper. The only risk of holding `std::sync::Mutex` across an `.await` on a single-threaded runtime is deadlock (the same thread tries to re-acquire the lock), not a runtime panic on drop.
rust.badFix: because `std::sync::Mutex` is not designed to be held across a context switch and will deadlock on a single-threaded runtime if the same thread attempts to re-acquire the lock while the guard is live.

scala.claim: Scala 2 banned case-to-case inheritance outright; Scala 3 maintains the restriction.
scala.problem: Scala 2 does NOT ban case-to-case inheritance. Since Scala 2.11 it issues a deprecation warning ('case class X has case ancestor Y, but case-to-case inheritance is prohibited') but still compiles. Scala 3 is where the hard error was introduced. The directionality is also wrong: Scala 3 tightened the restriction, it did not merely maintain a Scala 2 ban.
scala.badFix: Scala 2 issues a deprecation warning for case-to-case inheritance but still compiles; Scala 3 promoted this to a hard compile error. Use a sealed trait hierarchy or composition instead.

scala.claim: break the cycle by using `lazy given` so the forward reference resolves after all instances are registered
scala.problem: `lazy given` is not valid Scala 3 syntax. The `given` keyword does not accept a `lazy` modifier in the Scala 3 grammar. The idiomatic way to defer a `given` definition is to use a `def`-based alias given or place a `lazy val` inside the given body, but `lazy given X = ...` does not compile.
scala.badFix: break the cycle by using a `def`-based alias given (e.g., `given myInstance: MyType = lazyComputed`) or placing `lazy val` inside the given body, so the forward reference resolves after all instances are registered

snowflake.claim: By default it fails and skips the entire file (ON_ERROR=ABORT_FILE).
snowflake.problem: ABORT_FILE is not a valid ON_ERROR value in Snowflake and it is not the default. The actual default for COPY INTO is ABORT_STATEMENT, which aborts the entire statement (not just a file) on the first error. Card 47 in this same file correctly states 'ABORT_STATEMENT (the default for bulk COPY)'. This followup contradicts that card and states a non-existent option.
snowflake.badFix: By default it fails the entire COPY statement (ON_ERROR=ABORT_STATEMENT). You can set ON_ERROR=CONTINUE to load valid rows and skip bad ones, or ON_ERROR=SKIP_FILE to skip the whole file on any error. Always check VALIDATE() or query load history to find rejected rows.

spark.claim: "accent": "fix" on the 'Before — N files per dt directory' code snippet
spark.problem: The 'Before' snippet demonstrates the problematic pattern (writing without pre-repartition, producing many files). It should have accent 'bug' to visually flag it as the bad example, matching the pattern used throughout the file (e.g. cards 4, 5, 9). Instead it carries 'fix', the same label as the correct 'After' snippet, so both appear as correct patterns.

supply-chain.claim: safety stock at 95% drops from roughly 351 to about 170 units — a 51% reduction
supply-chain.problem: The '351 units' baseline came from the main card's LT=14 example. The followup scenario is LT=30→7. Using the full formula promoted in the card (SS = Z × √(LT_avg × σ²_demand + D_avg² × σ²_LT)) with the same parameters (σ_demand=20, D_avg=100, σ_LT=2, Z=1.645), the correct baseline at LT=30 is ~375 units, and at LT=7 it is ~340 units — only a ~9% reduction, not 51%. The 51% figure is obtained only from the simpler single-term formula (Z × σ_demand × √LT) without the LT-variance term, which contradicts the formula the card is specifically teaching. The writer mixed the full-formula result for LT=14 (351) with a simple-formula ratio for LT=30→7.
supply-chain.badFix: Both safety stock components shrink: LT_avg × σ²_demand drops from 30×σ² to 7×σ², and the σ²_LT term also typically shrinks because shorter lead times tend to have lower absolute variance. Using the simpler formula (SS = Z × σ_demand × √LT_avg) — appropriate when lead-time variance is negligible after the improvement — safety stock at 95% drops from roughly 180 units (LT=30) to about 87 units (LT=7) — a ~52% reduction. When lead-time variance also shrinks substantially, the full formula produces a comparable improvement. This quantifies the working capital value of supplier lead time reduction, which is a core input when justifying near-shoring or dual-sourcing decisions.

tableau.claim: 2024.2 was the last Desktop version that supported it
tableau.problem: Tableau Desktop stopped creating .tde extracts around 2020-2021 (approximately Desktop 2021.1). The assertion that Desktop 2024.2 was the last version to support .tde is a suspiciously precise claim that does not match known Tableau release history and has the hallmarks of an AI-hallucinated version number.
Same issue as the main answer: the version number '2024.2' attached to the .tde deprecation is almost certainly incorrect. .tde creation was removed from Tableau Desktop years before 2024.2.

tableau.claim: Tableau recommends starting with 2 VizQL processes per physical or virtual CPU (cores / 2 as a rough guide, capped at 4 per node for most workloads).
tableau.problem: '2 VizQL processes per CPU' and 'cores / 2 as a rough guide' are contradictory. On an 8-core node, '2 per CPU' yields 16 processes while 'cores / 2' yields 4. Tableau's actual guidance is approximately cores / 2 total VizQL processes per node, not 2 per core. The parenthetical correctly states 'cores/2' but the opening clause states the opposite.
tableau.badFix: Tableau recommends starting with roughly cores / 2 VizQL Server processes per node as a rule of thumb (e.g., 4 processes on an 8-core node), capped by the concurrency you actually observe at peak. Each VizQL process handles one session thread at a time, so process count sets the max concurrent interactive sessions on that node.

tableau.claim: Use `POST /api/{version}/sites/{siteId}/workbooks/{workbookId}/move` to move workbooks to the Archive project.
tableau.problem: There is no /move sub-resource in the Tableau REST API. Moving a workbook to a different project is done by calling `PUT /api/{version}/sites/{siteId}/workbooks/{workbookId}` with an updated `project.id` in the request body — the same Update Workbook endpoint used for certification. The /move endpoint is fabricated.
tableau.badFix: Use `PUT /api/{version}/sites/{siteId}/workbooks/{workbookId}` with an updated `project.id` body field to move workbooks to the Archive project.

typescript.claim: The `const` modifier on type parameters is not supported on arrow function type-parameter lists in the same way (it was a parser/feature limitation), so you generally apply it on function declarations or method signatures.
typescript.problem: This repeats the incorrect claim from the main answer. TypeScript 5.0 explicitly supports `<const T>` on arrow functions. The parenthetical '(it was a parser/feature limitation)' describes a past or TSX-specific concern, not a present general restriction. The instruction to 'apply it on function declarations or method signatures' is therefore incorrect guidance.

typescript.claim: use the throwing overload `inject(key, defaultFactory, true)` which returns `T` (non-optional) but throws at runtime if no provider is found
typescript.problem: Vue 3's `inject` has no throwing overload. The third-argument `true` signature is `inject(key, defaultFactory, treatDefaultAsFactory: true): T`, which means 'call `defaultFactory()` and use its return value as the default'. The factory is invoked when no provider exists, returning a fallback value — it does NOT throw. Callers following this advice would receive a computed default, not a runtime error, and would never discover an unset injection key through this path.
typescript.badFix: Pass a default value as the second argument to `inject(key, defaultValue)`, or use the factory overload `inject(key, defaultFactory, true)` which calls `defaultFactory()` and returns its result as the default when no provider is found. To throw on a missing injection, check the result manually: `const svc = inject(key); if (!svc) throw new Error('AuthKey not provided')`.

vectordb.claim: Graph edge storage is approximately M × 8 bytes × n (for float32 edges stored bidirectionally).
vectordb.problem: HNSW graph edges store neighbor node indices, not float32 values. Each neighbor ID is an int32 (4 bytes), not a float32. The formula M × 8 bytes is also internally inconsistent with every other card in this file, which uses 4 × M × n bytes for the graph. The parenthetical 'float32 edges' is factually wrong in both the type (should be int32) and the stated size (4 bytes, not 8). At M=16 and n=10M: 4×M×n = 640 MB, while M×8×n = 1.28 GB — a 2× inflation.
vectordb.badFix: Graph edge storage is approximately M × 4 bytes × n (for int32 neighbor-index entries). For n=10M vectors with M=16: ~640 MB for the graph, plus n × d × 4 bytes for the raw vectors (e.g., 30 GB for d=768).

vectordb.claim: 3-large achieves higher MTEB retrieval benchmark scores (avg ~54 vs ~62)
vectordb.problem: The numbers are materially wrong. On the MTEB English retrieval subset (NDCG@10), text-embedding-3-small scores approximately 44–45 and text-embedding-3-large approximately 55–56 as of 2024–2025. The card's figures (~54 for 3-small, ~62 for 3-large) overstate 3-small by ~10 points and overstate 3-large by ~6 points. The direction is correct (3-large is higher) but a candidate quoting '54 vs 62' will be challenged by any interviewer who has looked at the MTEB leaderboard.

voice-ai.claim: async for transcript in dg.listen.asynclive(audio_stream, LiveOptions(model='nova-2')):
voice-ai.problem: In Deepgram Python SDK v3, dg.listen.asynclive is a class accessor, not a callable that accepts (audio_stream, LiveOptions) and returns an async iterator. The correct v3 pattern is event-callback-based: you obtain a connection via dg.listen.asynclive.v('1'), register handlers with connection.on(LiveTranscriptionEvents.Transcript, handler), then call await connection.start(options) and send audio chunks via await connection.send(). Iterating over dg.listen.asynclive(audio_stream, ...) will throw AttributeError.
